thread_title: NVME GEN 5 Drive - Experience Sharing
post_content:
Samsung 9100 PRO 4TB.








Do keep in mind the results should be 20% faster as there is design flaw with Arrow Lake trace from the CPU to the GEN5 M2 slot resulting in high latency and performance penalty. I'm not sure if high D2D overclock can make it faster as the closed beta D2D workaround does not work for me.



I don't really mind the speed because I bought it with intention to add more capacity for storage and I have no use case for even faster storage.

thread_title: 4K Gaming PC Advice
quoted_text:
Preferably a mATX or Mini ITX built. Would need to install a PCIe 10G NIC
Click to expand...
post_content:
if u dun need diy, suggest u get something like this: https://www.invaderpc.com/product/specs05/ and change the case as necessary. even with that and a 2tb ssd upgrade, it ends up over $100 cheaper vs aftershock, and u get:

- a decent midrange mobo

- slightly faster ram (u can still upgrade this with invader)

- a decent psu vs unknown thermalright unit

arrow lake (intel ultra 200 series) is objectively worse for gaming (previous gen 7000 series beats out 265k in cpu limited gaming) and more expensive vs ryzen; cpu is less critical for gaming at higher resolutions like 4k but why spend more?

pretty difficult to get a 10g nic with an itx build since u dont get more pcie slots other than the main one for gpu. matx is a pretty solid place to be imo.

thread_title: 4K Gaming PC Advice
post_content:
Honestly, I won't go for 5080. The performance difference between 5070ti and 5080 is like 15%. Not like you're getting extra vram or extra features as well.



But the price difference?



Given these 2 base builds with similar specs with just different gpu -



5070ti: https://www.invaderpc.com/product/specs08/ @ $1899



5080: https://www.invaderpc.com/product/specs21/ @ $2409



The price difference is a massive $510 for a 15% increase in performance. That's a massive 26% price premium.



Would highly recommend the 5070ti for you.

Based on the article above, here is my opinion:



Main point: still manufactured on 5nm TSMC fab process, but enhanced.

 +30% above RTX4000 series in first year of launch because it will be selling concurrently with existing stock of RTX4000 series. Then price cut in 2nd year to match RTX4000 pricing after the old stock are sold out.

post_content:
My opinion is that even if Nvidia moved to 3nm, the uplift from 5nm is not going to be significant. You donâ€™t have to look too far in terms of how well 3nm performs. Just take a look at Appleâ€™s M2 vs M3. The latter is faster, but also requires higher peak power draw. I think TSMC is hitting a speed bump in terms of shrinking transistors and not unexpected. For all you know, the 3nm is nothing more than marketing fluff with the underlying not much smaller than their 5nm. 



Back to the expected performance uplift, I donâ€™t think this comes as a surprise. The retail Ada GPUs were given VIP treatment by Nvidia as they are manufactured on a cutting edge node. Ampere was given the usual treatment of getting cheaper mature node. So from Samsungâ€™s 10nm up to TSMCâ€™s 5nm is almost a 2 to 3 node improvement given that Samsung tend to fall behind TSMC. That benefit itself allowed Ada to frog leap Ampere with ease with beefier hardware and massive increase in clock speed. With that big improvement in node gone, we are back to the norm. Given how demanding current games are, Nvidia is likely going to double down on software to boost performance. I wonâ€™t be surprised they add more AI features in their marketing and make it RTX 5xxx exclusive.

thread_title: Nvidia RTX5000 up to +30% better than RTX4000 (only!)
post_content:
I'm doing this to plan the depreciation path of my GPU purchase.



Solution:


2024: Buy RTX4080 Super (with 3yr to 5yr warranty) for 4k gaming
2025-2027: Skip Blackwell GPU since it is still made on TSMC 5nm process
2028: Upgrade to RTX6000 series. Sell RTX4080 for 66% loss.
(I'm not buying RTX4090, too hot and too much electricity power).



Reasoning:

Assuming the best AMD RX8000 (RDNA4) gpu is launched in the mid of first year of Blackwell GPU, and it barely matches RTX4080, here is my prediction of the Blackwell GPU ranking and launch timing based on Nvidia's profit maximisation principle:




RTX5090 (replace RTX4090 that sells out easily, and will launch with 10% to 30% price bump)
RTX4090


RTX5080 (replace RTX4080 Super, 24GB, with 10% to 30% price bump)


RTX5070 (launch in the 2nd year Blackwell GPU, just match RTX4080, not overtake it)
RTX4080


Top AMD RX8000 gpu (estimated performance here)


RTX4070 TI Super (price will be cut)
RTX5060 (launched in 2nd year of Blackwell)
RTX4070
RTX4060
RTX4070 TI Super and lower will be exposed to AMD price competition.



[YouTube Video] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Hbx4AUcQ5do



Computex 2024 (4 - 7 Jun) will provide more clarity with the anticipated AMD's update about RDNA4 GPUs.



IMO, Blackwell GPUs are more appealing to current RTX3000 series owners wishing to upgrade.

thread_title: Nvidia RTX5000 up to +30% better than RTX4000 (only!)
post_content:
I have always gone for the X080 series until I realized how much faster the 4090 was over the 4080 (helped in part by Nvidia's stinginess in VRAM except for its Titan-class X090 series). I have been PC gaming for decades and I've realized that getting flagship CPU/GPUs is the reason people say "Pay more buy once, Pay less buy twice". That hefty sum is a big investment but it also means not bothering about upgrades in the next 3 years (although human nature dictates that one would want the next latest/greatest when a new flagship surfaces in 2 years time). 



My last PC upgrade was a Strix Z790-E + i9-13900K + 4090. If I were to get the 5090 (if the price is not too prohibitive), it would probably be the swan song for my current rig as any further upgrade would require a new motherboard + CPU + RAM. I still have to worry if my i9-13900K at 5.5Ghz would pose too much of a bottleneck for the 5090. Another problem is I am not justifying such a hefty invesment as I noticed I have not been finishing, or even playing, all the new games I have bought in the last 6 months so it could be a swan song for the hobby and not just the hardware lol. 



I don't think the white haired dude in the black leather jacket is interested in catering to us loyal Nvidia PC gamers now that AI chips are transforming Nvidia into a trillion dollar IT empire, even overshadowing the mighty Apple.

post_content: How are you guy's experience buying externally from Amazon/Newegg etc? I saw keyboards selling much cheaper at Amazon but the lack of local warranty is worrying, especially when I have bad experience with Razer products which I have sent for RMA (replacement) many times in the past. The price may be attractive but nothing beats having local warranty especially when Ban Leong's approach to Razer RMAs is to simply verify your keyboard's serial number, a quick test to check that there is a fault, and then they just go to the storeroom to give you a sealed brand new replacement. High-end GPUs local warranty is even more important which is why I always choose Zotac for GPUs due to their 3+2 years extended warranty.

I only usually get NVME or RAMs not from Amazon/Newegg. Can't beat their prices and usually those items don't really fail. Failure does happen like my previous Gskill DDR5 rams but RMA was so easy by just opening a ticket to Gskill. You then ship your rams to them in Taiwan at your own cost and they will send back a brand new set of rams and cover the shipping back to you.



GPUs and all is more hassle to RMA but it really depends on your appetite for risk. I personally don't for big ticket items.

thread_title: Nvidia RTX5000 up to +30% better than RTX4000 (only!)
quoted_text:
The energy efficiency part is quite interesting.  More than 300X reduction in energy required to train a GPT AI in just a few generations from Pascal to Blackwell.  This directly translates to efficiency in time required to train an AI.
Click to expand...
post_content:
It is no surprise to be honest. The number looks very high, but in truth, there are a number of factors to it,

1. Die shrink from I believe 14nm to now, allows them to squeeze in more Tensor cores to deliver better machine learning performance



2. During Pascal, the focus is around using the CUDA cores to perform suitable tasks and not so much about AI. So now that the focus have changed, they are sprucing up what is more critical for AI training. In very layman terms, its like transitioning from a brute force AI training solution to one that is more AI training specialized/ optimized.

post_content: I expect the RTX 5080 to have very limited performance improvement against the current RTX 4080 Super. Historically, the CUDA cores tend to increase when moving to a new generation. But RTX 4080 Super to 5080 hardly resulted in any CUDA core increase. So any improvement will be derived from IPC improvement and also the increase in memory bandwidth from the GDDR7.

thread_title: Nvidia RTX5000 up to +30% better than RTX4000 (only!)
post_content:
pricing leak



about S$3500 for 5090 and S$1860 for 5080



What we've all been waiting for, might just have appeared and what we're talking about is of course the pricing of NVIDIA's upcoming graphics cards. @wxnod has posted a single screenshot on X/Twitter of what could be the MSRP of the RTX 5090 and RTX 5080 in China. The MSRP of the RTX 4080 was 9,499 RMB and the RTX 5080 appears to be not that much higher, at 9,999 RMB, but this still equates to about US$1,370, although do note that there's 13 percent sales tax/VAT in China.



Now as for the RTX 5090, things won't be as rosy. The RTX 4090 had an MSRP of 12,999 RMB in China and the RTX 5090 comes in at an insane 18,999 RMB or US$2,600. That's a price hike of a not insignificant 46 percent over the RTX 4090 and this might make it the most expensive consumer graphics card ever released. We'd suggest taking these prices with a helping of NaCl just to be on the safe side. The cards are expected to be available some time in January according to the screenshot.

thread_title: Nvidia RTX5000 up to +30% better than RTX4000 (only!)
post_content:
Inno3D RTX5090 32GB



https://videocardz.com/newz/exclusive-first-look-at-geforce-rtx-5090-with-32gb-gddr7-memory








Inn03D is set to launch its new iChill X3 design. This card, the GeForce RTX 5090 iChiII X3, is a flagship Blackwell-based model from this company. It features a massive 3.5-slot thick cooler, which is actually highlighted as one of its key features. 



Currently, only one picture of the card is available, so the number of power connectors cannot be confirmed. However, the packaging reveals that it comes with 32GB of GDDR7 memory. This is the first confirmed detail about the RTX 5090's memory configuration. This confirms that the flagship RTX 50 model will indeed have twice the capacity of the 5080 model.

post_content:
Man, the 5090 looks even thicker than the 4090. Like almost reaching 4 slots. At this rate, I think future high end motherboards no need more than one pcie slot since they would be mostly paired with high end gpu models that end up covering them all.



Can't wait to see sg price for it. At 1999 US msrp, it means that lowest also starting 2.7k sgd. And that's before gst, distro premium, etc. I think starting point for even the "lowest end" 5090 is gonna be 3k sgd+. "Premium" models like the Asus Strix line will likely break the 4k barrier.



But at least Asus is trying to justify its premium by switching to PCM as the TIM for its high end GPU model. All the other brands still seem to be using regular paste for their 5090s.



:(

thread_title: Nvidia RTX5000 up to +30% better than RTX4000 (only!)
quoted_text:
whatcha doing with it? the extent of my homelab is only a rack mounted ubiquiti network with a 70+tb unraid server
Click to expand...
post_content:
Mainly as AI server clusters and AI development projects/POC. I have deployed a few of these self-hosted AI servers (homelab & worklab) alongside my team to automate their coding workflow (Eg. code-gemma). You can also use these self-hosted (AI) APIs for integration with work/industrial applications.

For home-labers, I think with the current consumer hardware most can easily spin up their very own self-hosted LLMs (GPT-like). Then move on to explore more AI territory from there. If you are curious enough, you can even integrate these free LLMs with Whisper on Home Assistant and run local "Google-like voice assistants"  ;)â€‹

thread_title: Nvidia RTX5000 up to +30% better than RTX4000 (only!)
post_content:
I'm disapointed that Nvidia has chosen to go down such a misleading path. Previous series, they stretched performance targets for games. That's minor. Now, it's just straight up misleading IMO. It makes it sound like the 5070 is the hardware equivalent of the 4090 - ie native vs native. They never even bothered to clarify what they meant by the 5070 having "4090 performance". Cause if it is an apple to apple comparsion where both are fully utilising whatever software tricks (dlss. fg, etc) available to them, the 4090 would invariably still come out on top by a wide margin.



Like that, then nearly every card they make can also claim to be the "equivalent" of the highest model. 4080 is "4090 performance". Even the 5070 can claim to be "5090 performance". But of course they won't do the latter as it basically makes no sense for the 5090 to even exist as a product, let alone justify its price.



The claim doesn't make any economic sense either. Nvidia is not one to cannibalise the pricing of their old models. In fact, they want to maintain them as high as possible. A usd 570 card being able to match the 4090 in terms of hardware? Then who would still want to buy the remaining stocks of 4090 or even the lower tier (4080 and below) models?



:(

If talk about 9070xt being on par with 7900xt turns out to be true, then I don't think it will be that low.



The reason I think so is because they don't want to end up affecting the sales of their remaining stock of 7900xt. If it is cheaper than the 7900xt but offers the same performance, then no one is going to buy the 7900xt unless its price is reduced to below the 9070xt. I also assume that they are going to continue making the 7900xt until their UDNA series comes about.



If their intention is to phase out the 7900xt and have the 9070xt replace it, then the 9070xt would likely also take up the 7900xt pricing tier (ie 700 ish) in the hierachy.



In any case, I don't expect below 600 usd for it.

thread_title: Need advise on NAS storage
quoted_text:
No idea about what is a good NAS
First time exploring and wanting to get a good brand for personal storage
Can anyone recommend and how do i start?
Thank you
Click to expand...
post_content:
General rules when consider getting a NAS:â€‹
Room for upgrade: If you need a 2-bay, get a 4-bay. You will likely upgrade when you familiar yourself with a NAS or create additional backups/mirrors. Give yourself more room (bays)â€‹
CPU: Get one with x86-64 hardware. preferably one from Intel, which supports QuickSync for transcoding. Powerful CPU allows you to do more, like creating VMs or multiple containers running services. Do consider your needs before committing to the CPU of your choiceâ€‹
Fast networking: Go for one with at least 2.5G networking, skip the 1Gâ€‹
HW lock in: Get one without hardware lock-in like the "Syno....."â€‹
Software: Go watch some reviews on YT and compare the different NAS software and which one best suits your use case in terms of user friendliness and support for popular containerisation like Dockerâ€‹
Weigh the pros and cons, you will find the "good NAS" you need.â€‹

thread_title: Need advise on NAS storage
post_content:
1. External HDD with thunderbolt/usb4 speeds.



2. External HDD cases with 2-4 bays and RAID 0/1/5. Also with thunderbolt/usb4 speeds



These are mainly and solely for the purpose of storage. No need to share the HDD, internally or externally, no use for any apps/functions of NAS.



Get high quality HDD, like the enterprise ones. Highly durable and long lasting.

thread_title: Memory bottlenecking me?
post_content:
Hmm? Which game uses almost 32 GB ram. Memory leak? Run Rammap and see who's hogging. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/sysinternals/downloads/rammap



Once I had the same issue, and my PC was beeping, thought it was game sound effect. Game is Wuthering Wave during its PC launch, non Steam with launcher. PC is powerful enough to play "smoothly" but the beeping annoys the living crap out of me. On checking, I find it was memory leaking and used up all my memory and more(page file exploded), stopped playing until they patched it. Beeping is gone if I deliberately purge the dead memory in the RAM with RAMMap, but have to do it once every 3 hours or so.

You shouldn't mix RAM, but your choice of RAM suggest you only require serviceable RAM.

Since you got Crucial but the speed is only JEDEC speed of DDR5-4800. 

You won't be able to get the maximum performance of the Intel i5 13600k 

which supoorts DDR5-5600. 



You can consider to get two of this. 



2) Which SSD are you using? 

I remember was Timetec or something? 

If the SSD is HMB/DRAMless, portion of RAM is used for cache. 



3) Windows memory leak 

Not possible for a normal desktop to hit more than 32GB. 

Either memory leak, SSD having issues thus lagging and Windows 

having isssues. 

Especially recent Win update causing a lot of PC SSD to fail. 



To isolate this issue recommend is to get another SSD than install Windows. 

Along with better ram like 2 X 16GB or 2 X 32GB. 



In before this thread became Taobao thread. 

And yes sad to say current DRAM prices have went up. 

As DDR4 has ended production thus DDR5 price will soon go up.

thread_title: Memory bottlenecking me?
post_content:
Most consumer motherboard (including gaming ones) are terrible in supporting 4x memory DIMMs. Normally you might need to lower the speed to JEDEC and in some instances even need to increase the latency to run it stable (24/7). Furthermore, same model of RAM can still be different when bought at different batches, revisions at different period of time, which adds on to the variable.

I recommend going with 2x 32GB memory DIMMs if you want to upgrade your memory. If you just have to go with 4 sticks, then it is better to stick to your motherboard QVL list, which is still not a guarantee too.â€‹
